claim_text: Chlamydia trachomatis is most prevalent in the UK among sexually-experienced individuals aged 16 to 24.

runs.response_text:
VERDICT: SUPPORT

Reasoning: The abstract from "Prevalence, risk factors, and uptake of interventions for sexually transmitted infections in Britain: findings from the National Surveys of Sexual Attitudes and Lifestyles (Natsal)" reports that chlamydia prevalence was 3.1% in women and 2.3% in men aged 16-24 years, which is higher than in older age groups and among individuals with more partners. This directly supports the claim that Chlamydia trachomatis is most prevalent in the UK among sexually experienced individuals aged 16 to 24.

VERDICT: SUPPORT
The abstract from the Natsal-3 study directly supports the claim by stating that "Chlamydia prevalence was 1.5% (95% CI 1.1-2.0) in women and 1.1% (0.7-1.6) in men. Prevalences in individuals aged 16-24 years were 3.1% (2.2-4.3) in women and 2.3% (1.5-3.4) in men." This directly indicates that Chlamydia trachomatis is most prevalent among sexually-experienced individuals aged 16 to 24 in the UK.

VERDICT: SUPPORT

The abstract reports that chlamydia prevalence in individuals aged 16-24 years was 3.1% (2.2-4.3) in women and 2.3% (1.5-3.4) in men, indicating it is most prevalent among this age group. This directly supports the claim that chlamydia trachomatis is most prevalent among sexually-experienced individuals aged 16 to 24 in the UK.

VERDICT: SUPPORT
The abstract states that "Prevalences in individuals aged 16-24 years were 3.1% (2.2-4.3) in women and 2.3% (1.5-3.4) in men," directly supporting the claim that Chlamydia trachomatis is most prevalent in the UK among sexually-experienced individuals aged 16 to 24.
